name: terraform
context: HashiCorp Terraform -- infrastructure as code using HCL to provision, plan, and manage cloud resources declaratively
info: Terraform is HashiCorp's open-source infrastructure as code (IaC) tool that lets agents define cloud and on-premises resources in human-readable HCL configuration files, then provision and manage them through a consistent workflow: write, init, plan, apply. Terraform tracks real-world infrastructure in a state file and uses a provider plugin model to interact with hundreds of cloud platforms, SaaS services, and APIs. The module system enables reusable, composable infrastructure components. This ecosystem tree covers the CLI, HCL language, state management, providers, modules, workspaces, and troubleshooting.
body:
# Terraform Ecosystem

Terraform is an infrastructure as code tool that lets you define cloud and on-premises resources in declarative configuration files. Instead of clicking through a console or writing imperative scripts, you describe the desired end state and Terraform figures out how to reach it. Agents need Terraform to reproducibly create, modify, and destroy infrastructure across any cloud provider.

## Why Agents Need Terraform

- **Declarative** -- describe what you want, not how to get there
- **Idempotent** -- running the same config twice produces the same result
- **Plan before apply** -- always preview changes before they take effect
- **Multi-cloud** -- one tool, one workflow for AWS, GCP, Azure, and hundreds more
- **State-aware** -- Terraform knows what it manages and can detect drift
- **Composable** -- modules let you build reusable infrastructure packages

## Installation

```bash
# macOS (Homebrew)
brew tap hashicorp/tap
brew install hashicorp/tap/terraform

# Ubuntu/Debian
wget -O - https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com/gpg | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg
echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg] https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com $(lsb_release -cs) main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/hashicorp.list
sudo apt update && sudo apt install terraform

# Verify installation
terraform version
```

## Core Workflow

Every Terraform operation follows the same four-step cycle:

```
  write  -->  init  -->  plan  -->  apply
    |                                 |
    +---- modify & repeat <-----------+
```

1. **Write** -- create `.tf` files describing the desired infrastructure
2. **Init** -- `terraform init` downloads providers and initializes the backend
3. **Plan** -- `terraform plan` computes what changes are needed (add, modify, destroy)
4. **Apply** -- `terraform apply` executes the plan against the real infrastructure

```bash
# Typical workflow
mkdir my-infra && cd my-infra

# Write configuration
cat > main.tf << 'EOF'
terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "data" {
  bucket = "my-data-bucket-unique-id"

  tags = {
    Environment = "dev"
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
  }
}
EOF

# Initialize, plan, apply
terraform init
terraform plan -out=tfplan
terraform apply tfplan
```

## HCL Basics

HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL) is Terraform's native syntax. See [[terraform/hcl]] for full coverage.

```hcl
# Variables
variable "region" {
  type        = string
  default     = "us-east-1"
  description = "AWS region to deploy into"
}

# Resources -- the core building block
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  tags = {
    Name = "web-server"
  }
}

# Outputs -- expose values after apply
output "instance_ip" {
  value = aws_instance.web.public_ip
}

# Data sources -- read existing infrastructure
data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["099720109477"]

  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-*-amd64-server-*"]
  }
}

# Locals -- computed values
locals {
  common_tags = {
    Project   = "my-app"
    ManagedBy = "terraform"
  }
}
```

## Provider Model

Providers are plugins that let Terraform manage specific platforms. Each provider offers resources and data sources for its platform. See [[terraform/providers]] for details.

```hcl
terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
    google = {
      source  = "hashicorp/google"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
    azurerm = {
      source  = "hashicorp/azurerm"
      version = "~> 3.0"
    }
  }
}
```

Popular providers: `hashicorp/aws`, `hashicorp/google`, `hashicorp/azurerm`, `hashicorp/kubernetes`, `hashicorp/helm`, `hashicorp/docker`, `hashicorp/github`, `hashicorp/cloudflare`, `hashicorp/datadog`.

## State Management

Terraform stores a mapping between your configuration and real-world resources in a state file (`terraform.tfstate`). This is the source of truth for what Terraform manages. See [[terraform/state]] for backends, locking, and best practices.

```hcl
# Remote state backend (recommended for teams)
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state"
    key            = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
    encrypt        = true
  }
}
```

**Critical rule**: never edit the state file manually. Use `terraform state` commands or `terraform import`.

## Module System

Modules are reusable packages of Terraform configuration. They accept input variables and produce outputs. See [[terraform/modules]].

```hcl
# Calling a module
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "5.5.0"

  name = "my-vpc"
  cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"

  azs             = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b"]
  private_subnets = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24"]
  public_subnets  = ["10.0.101.0/24", "10.0.102.0/24"]

  enable_nat_gateway = true
}

# Using module outputs
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  subnet_id = module.vpc.public_subnets[0]
  # ...
}
```

## Environment Variables

Terraform respects several environment variables:

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `TF_VAR_<name>` | Set input variable `<name>` (e.g. `TF_VAR_region=us-west-2`) |
| `TF_LOG` | Enable logging: `TRACE`, `DEBUG`, `INFO`, `WARN`, `ERROR` |
| `TF_LOG_PATH` | Write logs to a file instead of stderr |
| `TF_DATA_DIR` | Override the `.terraform` data directory |
| `TF_INPUT` | Set to `0` or `false` to disable interactive prompts |
| `TF_IN_AUTOMATION` | Set to any value to adjust output for CI/CD |
| `TF_CLI_ARGS` | Additional CLI arguments appended to every command |
| `TF_CLI_ARGS_<command>` | Additional CLI arguments for a specific command (e.g. `TF_CLI_ARGS_plan`) |
| `TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR` | Directory to cache provider plugins across projects |
| `TF_WORKSPACE` | Select the active workspace |

```bash
# Example: CI/CD pipeline environment
export TF_IN_AUTOMATION=true
export TF_INPUT=0
export TF_VAR_environment="staging"
export TF_LOG=WARN
export TF_LOG_PATH="/tmp/terraform.log"
export TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR="$HOME/.terraform.d/plugin-cache"
```

## Destroy

Tear down all infrastructure managed by the current configuration:

```bash
# Preview what will be destroyed
terraform plan -destroy

# Destroy all resources
terraform destroy

# Destroy a specific resource
terraform destroy -target=aws_s3_bucket.data
```

## File Structure

A typical Terraform project layout:

```
my-infra/
  main.tf          # Primary resources
  variables.tf     # Input variable declarations
  outputs.tf       # Output value declarations
  providers.tf     # Provider configuration and versions
  terraform.tfvars # Variable values (do NOT commit secrets)
  backend.tf       # Remote state backend configuration
  modules/         # Local reusable modules
    vpc/
      main.tf
      variables.tf
      outputs.tf
```

sections.title: Installation
sections.body: Install Terraform via Homebrew on macOS (`brew install hashicorp/tap/terraform`), via APT on Ubuntu/Debian using the HashiCorp repository, or download the binary directly from releases.hashicorp.com. Verify with `terraform version`. For version management across projects, use `tfenv` or `asdf`.

sections.title: Core workflow: write, init, plan, apply
sections.body: Every Terraform operation follows write (create .tf files), init (download providers and initialize backend), plan (preview changes), apply (execute changes). Always save plans with `-out=tfplan` and apply saved plans for safety. In CI/CD, use `TF_IN_AUTOMATION=true` and `TF_INPUT=0`.

sections.title: HCL language basics
sections.body: HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language) is Terraform's declarative syntax. Core constructs: `resource` (infrastructure objects), `variable` (inputs), `output` (exposed values), `data` (read existing infrastructure), `locals` (computed values), `module` (reusable groups). See [[terraform/hcl]] for the full language reference.

sections.title: Provider plugin model
sections.body: Providers are plugins downloaded during `terraform init` that translate HCL resources into API calls. Declare required providers in a `required_providers` block with source and version constraints. Providers are published to the Terraform Registry. See [[terraform/providers]] for configuration and authentication.

sections.title: State management overview
sections.body: Terraform records the mapping between configuration and real resources in `terraform.tfstate`. Use remote backends (S3, GCS, Azure Blob, Terraform Cloud) for team collaboration with state locking. Never edit state manually -- use `terraform state mv`, `terraform state rm`, and `terraform import`. See [[terraform/state]].

sections.title: Module system
sections.body: Modules are reusable packages of Terraform configuration. Source from the Terraform Registry, Git repos, or local paths. Modules accept input variables and produce outputs. Pin versions for reproducibility. See [[terraform/modules]] for authoring, publishing, and composition patterns.

sections.title: Environment variables
sections.body: `TF_VAR_<name>` sets input variables. `TF_LOG` enables debug logging (TRACE/DEBUG/INFO/WARN/ERROR). `TF_LOG_PATH` redirects logs to a file. `TF_DATA_DIR` overrides the .terraform directory. `TF_INPUT=0` disables prompts. `TF_IN_AUTOMATION` adjusts output for CI. `TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR` caches providers across projects.

sections.title: Project file structure
sections.body: Standard layout: `main.tf` (primary resources), `variables.tf` (input declarations), `outputs.tf` (output declarations), `providers.tf` (provider config and versions), `terraform.tfvars` (variable values -- do not commit secrets), `backend.tf` (remote state). Group related resources logically. Use modules/ for local reusable components.

links.context: Initialize working directory -- download providers, configure backend, install modules
Preview changes -- compute diff between config and real infrastructure
Apply changes -- execute a plan to create, update, or destroy resources
State management -- inspect, move, remove, import tracked resources
Module system -- reusable infrastructure packages, registry, composition
Provider plugins -- configuration, authentication, version constraints
Kubernetes provider -- manage K8s resources with Terraform
GitHub Actions -- automate terraform plan/apply in CI/CD pipelines

related.note: AWS is the most commonly used Terraform provider for cloud infrastructure
Terraform can provision Kubernetes clusters and manage K8s resources
Terraform Docker provider manages containers, images, networks, and volumes

scripts.name: Terraform Init Plan Apply
scripts.description: Run the full Terraform workflow: initialize, plan, and apply with auto-approve
scripts.inline:
set -euo pipefail
echo '=== Terraform Init ===' && terraform init -input=false
echo '\n=== Terraform Plan ===' && terraform plan -out=tfplan -input=false
echo '\n=== Terraform Apply ===' && terraform apply -input=false tfplan
rm -f tfplan
echo '\n=== Done ==='

TF_IN_AUTOMATION.description: Set to true for CI/CD-friendly output

TF_LOG.description: Log level: TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR

scripts.name: Terraform Format and Validate
scripts.description: Format HCL files and validate configuration syntax
scripts.inline:
set -euo pipefail
echo '=== Formatting ===' && terraform fmt -recursive -diff
echo '\n=== Validating ===' && terraform init -backend=false -input=false > /dev/null 2>&1 && terraform validate
echo '\n=== All checks passed ==='

scripts.name: Terraform Destroy Target
scripts.description: Destroy a specific Terraform resource by target address
scripts.inline:
set -euo pipefail
if [ -z "${TARGET:-}" ]; then echo 'ERROR: TARGET is required (e.g. aws_s3_bucket.data)' && exit 1; fi
echo "=== Planning destruction of $TARGET ==="
terraform plan -destroy -target="$TARGET" -out=destroy.tfplan -input=false
echo '\nReview the plan above. Applying...'
terraform apply -input=false destroy.tfplan
rm -f destroy.tfplan
echo '\n=== Destroyed ==='

frontmatter.author: dojo-community
frontmatter.audience: developers, AI agents, DevOps engineers, SREs

author: dojo-community
